Scenario.title: CostaRicto Cyber Espionage Campaign Against Communication Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Communications Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting communication infrastructure operators, leveraging spearphishing, lateral movement, and data exfiltration to manipulate critical systems and steal sensitive operational data.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Intellectual property theft and disruption of communication networks

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: ["Social engineering", "Internet-facing assets", "Vulnerability", "Shared DB or directory", "Internal network", "Communication infrastructure"]

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: Adversaries harvested metadata from public cloud platforms and web services to identify communication infrastructure operators, employee contact details, and potential non-production interfaces for exploitation.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: A domain resembling a legitimate telecom provider was registered and used to host phishing pages designed to mimic official login portals, enabling credential harvesting.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2024-48248 [CWE-502]: Apache Log4j2 JNDI features used in configuration...
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 511: Infiltration of Software Development Environment
stage.description: Malicious Microsoft Office documents with embedded macros were delivered via spearphishing emails. The macro exploited a vulnerability in Apache Log4j2 (CVE-2024-48248) to execute arbitrary code on victim systems.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2024-50623 [CWE-89]: SQL injection vulnerability allowing command-line execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 108: Command Line Execution through SQL Injection
stage.description: An SQL injection attack exploited a vulnerable web application to inject PowerShell commands, enabling remote code execution and establishing a reverse shell connection.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A scheduled task was created to periodically execute a malicious script, ensuring persistent access even after reboot or user logout.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Unpatched remote management services (e.g., RDP) were exploited to escalate privileges, granting administrative access to core network devices.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 636: Hiding Malicious Data or Code within Files
stage.description: Malware payloads were encrypted and embedded within benign-looking log files to evade signature-based detection mechanisms.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: LSASS memory was dumped using Mimikatz to extract plaintext credentials stored in Windows systems, enabling access to domain accounts.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 643: Identify Shared Files/Directories on System
stage.description: The attackers mapped internal shares to locate sensitive directories containing customer data, encryption keys, and network topology diagrams.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 324: TCP (ISN) Sequence Predictability Probe
stage.description: Using stolen credentials, adversaries moved laterally across the network via RDP and SMB protocols to access additional systems.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 328: TCP 'RST' Flag Checksum Probe
stage.description: Communication logs, configuration files, and cryptographic keys were extracted from local systems for further analysis and potential exploitation.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data was compressed and exfiltrated over encrypted HTTPS channels to a command-and-control server hosted on a compromised cloud instance.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Critical routing tables and subscriber databases were altered to disrupt service availability and redirect traffic through malicious nodes.

Scenario.summary: The CostaRicto campaign executed a complex multi-stage attack against a communications provider, beginning with targeted spearphishing and escalating to network-wide disruption through credential theft, lateral movement, and data manipulation. The operation highlights the risks of unpatched software, weak access controls, and insufficient monitoring of privileged activities.
